name: Oscar Extended
description: Oscar, son of Oisín and grandson of Fionn mac Cumhaill, is a prominent hero in Irish mythology, famed for his unparalleled bravery, martial prowess, and unwavering loyalty. Often depicted as the ideal warrior, Oscar embodies the virtues of the Fianna, the legendary band of warriors in Irish lore. His lineage connects him directly to the heart of Irish heroism, inheriting the legendary skills and responsibilities of his grandfather and father. Oscar's deeds are celebrated in numerous tales, showcasing his exceptional skill in combat, his unwavering commitment to justice, and his profound respect for his comrades. He is a symbol of youthful vigor and righteous fury, always ready to defend the innocent and uphold the honor of the Fianna. Though his life was tragically cut short in the Battle of Gabhra, his legacy as a fearless and noble warrior continues to resonate within Irish mythology, serving as an inspiration for generations to come. Oscar's character is often contrasted with Diarmuid Ua Duibhne, showcasing different aspects of heroism within the Fianna. His death is a pivotal moment, signaling the decline of the Fianna's power and the end of an era. His story serves as a reminder of the ephemeral nature of glory and the enduring power of courage.
subtitle: The Valiant Son of Oisín

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Gabhra
keyMyths.description: Oscar's tragic death at the Battle of Gabhra marks a turning point in the history of the Fianna. He falls valiantly defending his people against the forces of Cairbre Lifechair, High King of Ireland.

keyMyths.title: Oscar's Prowess in Combat
keyMyths.description: Stories abound of Oscar's exceptional combat skills, often surpassing even those of his father, Oisín, and grandfather, Fionn. His strength and agility made him a formidable opponent in any battle.
keyMyths.source: The Pursuit of Diarmuid and Gráinne

keyMyths.title: Oscar's Defense of the Weak
keyMyths.description: Oscar was known for his unwavering commitment to protecting the innocent and defending those who could not defend themselves. This trait solidified his reputation as a noble hero.

sources.author: Standish Hayes O'Grady
sources.description: A collection of Irish texts with translations.
sources.text: A collection of Irish texts with translations.

sources.author: James Macpherson
sources.description: Though romanticized, it introduced Fenian Cycle stories to a wider audience.
sources.text: Though romanticized, it introduced Fenian Cycle stories to a wider audience.
